elements.sent-2411-001: As one of the automotive sensors that have emerged in recent years, 4D millimeter-wave radar has a higher resolution than conventional 3D radar and provides precise elevation measurements.
elements.sent-2411-002: But its point clouds are still sparse and noisy, making it challenging to meet the requirements of autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-003: Camera, as another commonly used sensor, can capture rich semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-004: As a result, the fusion of 4D radar and camera can provide an affordable and robust perception solution for autonomous driving systems.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, previous radar-camera fusion methods have not yet been thoroughly investigated, resulting in a large performance gap compared to LiDAR-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, they ignore the feature-blurring problem and do not deeply interact with image semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-007: To this end, we present a simple but effective multi-stage sampling fusion (MSSF) network based on 4D radar and camera.
elements.sent-2411-008: On the one hand, we design a fusion block that can deeply interact point cloud features with image features, and can be applied to commonly used single-modal backbones in a plug-and-play manner.
elements.sent-2411-009: The fusion block encompasses two types, namely, simple feature fusion (SFF) and multi-scale deformable feature fusion (MSDFF).
elements.sent-2411-010: The SFF is easy to implement, while the MSDFF has stronger fusion abilities.
elements.sent-2411-011: On the other hand, we propose a semantic-guided head to perform foreground-background segmentation on voxels with voxel feature re-weighting, further alleviating the problem of feature blurring.
elements.sent-2411-012: Extensive experiments on the View-of-Delft (VoD) and TJ4DRadset datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our MSSF.
elements.sent-2411-013: Notably, compared to state-of-the-art methods, MSSF achieves a 7.0\% and 4.0\% improvement in 3D mean average precision on the VoD and TJ4DRadSet datasets, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-014: It even surpasses classical LiDAR-based methods on the VoD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-015: Perception plays an important role in autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-016: It covers a lot of content, such as object detection\cite{langPointPillarsFastEncoders2019a,zhouVoxelNetEndtoEndLearning2018,shiPointRCNN3DObject2019a , tracking\cite{weng2020AB3DMOT}\cite{pang2022simpletrack , and segmentation\cite{jiang2024mwsis}\cite{zhang2024spatial .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Its purpose is to obtain the categories and 3D bounding boxes of critical objects (e.g., cars and pedestrians) in 3D scenes from sensor data.
elements.sent-2411-018: To achieve this, a wide variety of sensors can be used, such as LiDAR, radar, and camera.
elements.sent-2411-019: LiDAR can obtain high-precision point clouds of 3D scenes which well reflect the geometric information of objects, thereby achieving remarkable detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, it may be infeasible due to the high cost and susceptibility to adverse weather conditions such as rain and fog.
elements.sent-2411-021: Millimeter wave radar (refer to radar for convenience) is a more common vehicle sensor\cite{karangwa2023vehicle compared to LiDAR, with the advantages of low price, slight influence by rain and fog, and long detection range\cite{zhouDeepRadarPerception2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Before the emergence of 4D radar, conventional 3D radar, which can measure distance, azimuth, and Doppler, is primarily employed.
elements.sent-2411-023: Nevertheless, the lack of elevation measurements limits the perception capability of 3D radar.
elements.sent-2411-024: As a result, it can provide relatively dense three-dimensional point clouds like LiDAR, which is increasingly being recognized as an affordable alternative to LiDAR.
elements.sent-2411-025: Although 4D radar point clouds share numerous similarities with LiDAR point clouds, they are still sparse and noisy due to the limited ranging and angle resolution, multipath effects, and penetrability\cite{zhouDeepRadarPerception2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Hence, relying solely on 4D radar point clouds for 3D object detection has limitations in performance.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, there is substantial potential for performance improvement through fusion with other sensors.
elements.sent-2411-028: Meanwhile, it is worth noting that cameras are relatively cheap and easy to deploy, which can provide rich semantic information due to the high spatial resolution and ability to perceive the color and texture of objects.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=3.45in]{motivate4.pdf} \caption{An explanation of the feature-blurring problem. (a) elucidates the definitions of 2D foreground points, 3D foreground points, and 3D blurred points. (b) and (c) show the radar points and LiDAR points projected onto the image, respectively. The blue mask is the instance segmentation generated by SAM\cite{kirillovSegmentAnything2023}, the green points represent the 3D foreground points, and the red points represent the 3D blurred points. (d) illustrates quantitatively by averaging the ratio of the number of 3D foreground points to the number of 2D foreground points over around 200 instance masks for each class. ``\# 3D fore. pts'' represents the number of 3D foreground points, and ``\# 2D fore. pts'' represents the number of 2D foreground points.} \label{fig:motivate}
elements.sent-2411-029: Considering the above factors, some recent works attempt to integrate information from both radar and camera modalities.
elements.sent-2411-030: Some researchers\cite{kimCRNCameraRadar2023a,nabatiCenterFusionCenterbasedRadar2021,kimCRAFTCameraRadar3D2023 specifically design the fusion strategy of 3D radar point clouds and images, and have shown obvious improvement over single-modal baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-031: As an increasing number of 4D radar datasets\cite{meyer2019automotive,palffyMultiClassRoadUser2022,zhengTJ4DRadSet4DRadar2022,paek2022k are released, recent studies\cite{yaoRadarCameraFusionObject2023}\cite{xiongLXLLiDARExcluded2023b focus on the fusion method for 4D radar and camera.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: RCFusion\cite{yaoRadarCameraFusionObject2023 adopts orthographic feature transform (OFT)\cite{roddick2018orthographic to obtain image bird's eye view (BEV) feature maps and design an interactive attention module to fuse the BEV feature maps of point clouds and images.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: LXL\cite{xiongLXLLiDARExcluded2023b uses a depth-based sampling strategy to lift image features to 3D space with the help of the predicted radar occupancy.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Although existing 4D radar and camera fusion methods achieve good performance, they are mostly based on the BEV domain fusion framework designed for LiDAR-camera fusion, without explicitly considering the characteristics of radar.
elements.sent-2411-035: Consequently, the potential of radar-camera fusion has not been fully explored, leading to a large performance gap compared to LiDAR-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-036: Specifically, in contrast to the dense point clouds produced by LiDAR, the 4D radar captures fewer details in terms of geometry information.
elements.sent-2411-037: Hence, radar-camera fusion places a greater reliance on image semantic information compared to LiDAR-camera fusion.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, existing methods only fuse radar and camera information on the BEV, lacking sufficient feature interaction and neglecting the characteristic that radar relies more on semantic information from images.
elements.sent-2411-039: Meanwhile, serious feature-blurring problems may arise when projecting radar points onto the corresponding image for feature sampling.
elements.sent-2411-040: In Fig. \ref{fig:motivate , we explain this problem through visualization and statistics in the View-of-Delft (VoD) dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We first provide the definitions of 2D foreground points, 3D foreground points, and 3D blurred points, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:motivate (a).}
elements.sent-2411-042: A radar point is a 2D foreground point when its corresponding projection point falls within 2D instance masks, because it can sample image features of foreground objects.
elements.sent-2411-043: Further, if a 2D foreground point also falls within 3D ground truth boxes, it is defined as a 3D foreground point, otherwise, it is classified as a 3D blurred point.
elements.sent-2411-044: Fig. \ref{fig:motivate (b) and (c) show the 2D foreground points of radar and LiDAR, respectively, where the 3D foreground points are marked in green and the 3D blurred points in red.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Additionally, as the VoD dataset does not provide segmentation labels, we generate around 200 instance masks of each class with Segment Anything Model (SAM)\cite{kirillovSegmentAnything2023 as exemplified by the blue mask shown in Fig. \ref{fig:motivate (b) and (c).}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Interestingly, in 3D object detection, we mainly care about the 3D foreground points, and other points should be regarded as the background points.
elements.sent-2411-047: However, when sampling features on the image, all 2D foreground points capture features of foreground objects, which may lead to false alarms.
elements.sent-2411-048: We refer to this as the feature-blurring problem.
elements.sent-2411-049: For quantitative analysis, we further calculate the ratio of the number of 3D foreground points to the number of 2D foreground points for each instance.
elements.sent-2411-050: The result after averaging all instances of each class is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:motivate (d).}
elements.sent-2411-051: It is observed that radar has significantly lower ratios than LiDAR, especially for the pedestrian and cyclist categories.
elements.sent-2411-052: As for the car category, the gap between the two modalities is relatively small, due to the metal materials which weaken the penetration capability of radar.
elements.sent-2411-053: This observation indicates that the feature-blurring problem is severe under radar modality.
elements.sent-2411-054: To address the above problems, we propose a simple but effective multi-stage sampling fusion (MSSF) network based on 4D radar and camera.
elements.sent-2411-055: We fuse point cloud and image features more deeply in the backbone rather than using a separate image BEV branch.
elements.sent-2411-056: In particular, two types of fusion blocks based on image feature sampling are proposed to replace the blocks in some commonly used 3D sparse backbones.
elements.sent-2411-057: Through the deep interaction of point cloud features and image features achieved by the fusion blocks, MSSF can well identify 3D foreground points, thereby alleviating the feature-blurring problem.
elements.sent-2411-058: The cascading of multiple fusion blocks effectively leverages image features, further enhancing detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-059: Furthermore, we add a semantic-guided head to explicitly help the network distinguish 3D foreground points.
elements.sent-2411-060: Our approach can be easily applied to many single-modal 3D object detection networks based on voxel or pillar.
elements.sent-2411-061: Our contributions are summarized in four folds:.
elements.sent-2411-062: Taking into account the sparsity of 4D radar point clouds and the feature-blurring problem, we propose a simple but effective MSSF network.
elements.sent-2411-063: As an early attempt in the field, it provides a strong baseline for later research.
elements.sent-2411-064: We propose two general, plug-and-play voxel-image feature fusion blocks and insert them into some commonly used 3D sparse networks in multiple stages to achieve deep interaction between voxel and image features.
elements.sent-2411-065: It has good scalability and can be traded off as needed.
elements.sent-2411-066: A semantic-guided head is proposed to further alleviate the feature-blurring problem.
elements.sent-2411-067: On the one hand, the segmentation loss guides the network to distinguish between foreground and background points.
elements.sent-2411-068: On the other hand, the segmentation scores are used to re-weight the voxel features to play the role of attention.
elements.sent-2411-069: Experiments on the VoD and TJ4DRadset datasets show that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art radar-camera fusion methods by 7.0\% mAP and 4.0\% mAP, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-070: In particular, for the car category in the VoD dataset, our method achieves a substantial increase of 18.6\% AP compared to state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-071: Notably, our MSSF even surpasses some classic LiDAR-based models.
elements.sent-2411-072: The remainder of this article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-073: Section \ref{sec:related_work briefly reviews recent works on single-modal and multi-modal 3D object detection.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Section \ref{sec:method introduces our proposed model in detail.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In Section \ref{sec:exps , the experimental setups and implementation details are introduced, and the performance of our method is shown and analyzed.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Finally, our work is summarized in Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.subsection-2411-001: 3D Object Detection based on LiDAR/Radar Point Cloud
elements.sent-2411-077: LiDAR-based 3D object detection methods can be divided into\cite{mao3DObjectDetection2023a point-based\cite{shiPointRCNN3DObject2019a}\cite{yang3DSSDPointBased3D2020 , pillar-based\cite{langPointPillarsFastEncoders2019a , and voxel-based methods\cite{zhouVoxelNetEndtoEndLearning2018}\cite{yanSECONDSparselyEmbedded2018a}\cite{chenVoxelNeXtFullySparse2023a , etc., according to the representation of point cloud during network processing.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: In \cite{palmer2023reviewing , it is found that the point-based methods are less effective for 3D object detection under radar modality compared with pillar-based and voxel-based methods.}
elements.sent-2411-079: At present, the 3D object detection methods of radar point clouds are mostly improved based on pillar-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-080: RPFA-Net\cite{xuRPFANet4DRaDAR2021a replaces the pillar feature extractor in PointPillars\cite{langPointPillarsFastEncoders2019a with its proposed self-attention-based feature extraction layer, so that context information can be better perceived when encoding pillar features.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: RCFusion\cite{yaoRadarCameraFusionObject2023 believes that the Doppler and radar cross section (RCS) information of 4D radar is important, and codes the spatial, velocity, and RCS features separately when extracting pillar features.}
elements.sent-2411-082: SMURF\cite{liuSMURFSpatialMultiRepresentation2023a adds additional kernel density estimation features to the backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Both MVFAN\cite{yan2023mvfan and MUFASA\cite{peng2024mufasa leverage BEV and cylindrical coordinate views simultaneously to better capture radar point cloud features.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: RadarPillar\cite{musiat2024radarpillars applies self-attention to pillar features to enlarge the receptive field.}
elements.subsection-2411-002: 3D Object Detection based on LiDAR-Camera Fusion
elements.sent-2411-085: LiDAR and camera fusion strategies can be mainly divided into three categories, i.e., early fusion, middle fusion, and late fusion\cite{wang2023multi .}
elements.sent-2411-086: In early fusion\cite{voraPointPaintingSequentialFusion2020b, yinMultimodalVirtualPoint2021a, wangPointAugmentingCrossModalAugmentation2021, sindagiMVXNetMultimodalVoxelNet2019a , image information is embedded into the point cloud in various ways before the point cloud is input to the detection network, which is direct to implementation but lacks deep interaction with point cloud features and image features.}
elements.sent-2411-087: For instance, PointAugmenting\cite{wangPointAugmentingCrossModalAugmentation2021 and MVX-Net\cite{sindagiMVXNetMultimodalVoxelNet2019a both sample image features at a very early stage and adopt simple fusion strategies.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: In addition, middle fusion is more effective and has been studied more in recent years\cite{jiaoMSMDFusionFusingLiDAR2023a,liangBEVFusionSimpleRobust,liuBEVFusionMultiTaskMultiSensor2023,liLoGoNetAccurate3D2023a, huang2020epnet,yang2022deepinteraction .}
elements.sent-2411-089: It realizes the interaction and fusion of point cloud features and image features at the feature level.
elements.sent-2411-090: Among these methods, we noted that LoGoNet\cite{liLoGoNetAccurate3D2023a also projects the centroids of non-empty voxels into the image and uses multi-scale deformable cross-attention to fuse features.}
elements.sent-2411-091: However, the features after fusion are only used for the refinement of proposals.
elements.sent-2411-092: The detection results are limited by the quality of proposals generated by the single-modal detector in the first stage.
elements.sent-2411-093: Additionally, EPNet\cite{huang2020epnet and DeepInteraction\cite{yang2022deepinteraction also fuse image features through projection.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The former emphasizes point-level correspondence and mainly focuses on point-based backbones.
elements.sent-2411-095: The latter operates on point cloud BEV and image planes, and the correspondence between the two modalities is relatively coarse.
elements.sent-2411-096: Compared with these methods, we establish the correspondence between voxels of different resolutions and images from fine to coarse.
elements.sent-2411-097: As for the late fusion methods\cite{pang2020clocs which operate on the output of a LiDAR-based 3D object detector and a camera-based 2D object detector.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Insufficient feature interactions limit the potential of such methods.
elements.sent-2411-099: The existing research on LiDAR and camera fusion is of significant reference value when studying radar and camera fusion, since both 4D radar and LiDAR data can be presented in the form of point clouds.
elements.subsection-2411-003: 3D Object Detection based on Radar-Camera Fusion
elements.sent-2411-100: Some studies have pioneered the fusion of 3D radar and camera.
elements.sent-2411-101: CenterFusion\cite{nabatiCenterFusionCenterbasedRadar2021 extends radar points into pillars, which are associated with the proposals predicted from images in the view frustum and assist in 3D attribute estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-102: CRAFT\cite{kimCRAFTCameraRadar3D2023 employs the spatio-contextual fusion transformer to refine image proposals by radar measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-103: CRN\cite{kimCRNCameraRadar2023a adopts the BEV fusion framework and utilizes radar occupancy map to assist image view transformation and BEV feature fusion.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Until recently, some 4D radar datasets\cite{meyer2019automotive, palffyMultiClassRoadUser2022, zhengTJ4DRadSet4DRadar2022, paek2022k have been released, and there are relatively few studies focusing on the fusion of 4D radar and camera.}
elements.sent-2411-105: RCFusion\cite{yaoRadarCameraFusionObject2023 employs OFT\cite{roddick2018orthographic along with its shared attention encoder to generate image BEV feature maps.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: These feature maps are then fused with radar BEV features in an attention-based manner.
elements.sent-2411-107: LXL\cite{xiongLXLLiDARExcluded2023b utilizes a sampling-based method to lift image features, in which the radar occupancy grid predicted from the radar BEV feature map and the image depth prediction are used to assist the image BEV features generation.}
elements.sent-2411-108: UniBEVFusion\cite{zhao2024unibevfusion proposes Radar Depth Lift-Splat-Shoot, which incorporates additional radar data into the depth prediction process.}
elements.sent-2411-109: These methods are mainly based on the BEV domain fusion framework without explicitly considering the characteristics of radar, resulting in a large performance gap compared to LiDAR-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-110: The sparsity and noisiness of radar point clouds, along with the ill-posed nature of image depth estimation, can cause challenges in these methods when converting image features to voxel or BEV features.
elements.sent-2411-111: In this study, we propose a new fusion network.
elements.sent-2411-112: Specifically, we directly adopt radar points to sample image features, rather than doing explicit view transformation.
elements.sent-2411-113: By employing an effective multi-stage fusion strategy along with a semantic-guided head, multi-scale image features are fully utilized to achieve comprehensive and efficient fusion with point cloud features.
elements.sent-2411-114: Even so, our network remains simple and compatible while maintaining high performance.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=6in]{overall.pdf} \caption{The overall architecture of our MSSF. The image branch extracts features from images to obtain multi-scale feature maps. The voxel-image fusion backbone contains $n$ fusion blocks and $m$ ordinary blocks (abbreviated as ``Block'' in the figure), which absorb features from the image feature maps in multiple stages through our proposed fusion blocks. The non-empty voxel features of the last layer fusion block are fed into the semantic-guided head for foreground and background prediction, and the segmentation scores are utilized to weight the voxel features. The multi-scale features output by the last few blocks are passed through the 3D neck to obtain the fused BEV feature map which is sent to the detection head to obtain the final detection results.} \label{fig:overall}
elements.sent-2411-115: The overall network structure is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:overall , which can be divided into four parts: image branch, voxel-image fusion backbone, semantic-guided head, and detection head.}
elements.sent-2411-116: The image branch is employed to extract multi-scale features of images and encode image semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-117: In our method, we have no restrictions on the specific structure of the image branch.
elements.sent-2411-118: The voxel-image fusion backbone is one of the key components of the proposed method, which is responsible for extracting point cloud features and deeply fusing with image features output by the image branch.
elements.sent-2411-119: It consists of a cascade of several proposed fusion blocks and ordinary blocks.
elements.sent-2411-120: Through multi-stage fusion, point cloud features are deeply interacted with image features.
elements.sent-2411-121: The 3D neck aggregates the output of the last few blocks, obtaining the fused BEV feature map which contains information from both modalities.
elements.sent-2411-122: The semantic-guided head performs foreground and background segmentation on non-empty voxels with explicit supervision, helping the network to perceive 3D foreground points and further alleviate the feature-blurring problem.
elements.sent-2411-123: The segmentation scores are used to re-weight the voxel features to play the role of attention.
elements.sent-2411-124: The detection head utilizes the fused BEV feature map to predict the 3D bounding box (center, size, and orientation) and category of the object in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-125: More details for each part are introduced in the following subsections.
elements.sent-2411-126: Note that the fusion method introduced below is based on the voxel-based implementation.
elements.sent-2411-127: A pillar-based version is provided at the end of this section.
elements.sent-2411-128: The image branch is employed to extract multi-scale semantic features of the image, usually consisting of a backbone and a neck.
elements.sent-2411-129: The backbone extracts image features, and the neck fuses features with different receptive fields and sizes output by the backbone in different stages.
elements.sent-2411-130: The input of the image branch is an RGB image $\mathbf{I}\in \mathbb R^{H\times W \times 3}$, where $H$ and $W$ represent the height and width of the image, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-131: And the outputs are $n_I$ multi-scale feature maps $\mathbf{F _{I,i}\in \mathbb R^{H_i\times W_i \times C_i , i=1,2,\dots,n_I$, where $H_i$, $W_i$ and $C_i$ represent the height, width and channel dimensions of the $i$-th level, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: In our model, we adopt a classic configuration where the backbone is ResNet-50\cite{heDeepResidualLearning2016b and the neck is FPN\cite{linFeaturePyramidNetworks2017a .}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Fig. \ref{fig:overall shows a general voxel-image fusion backbone architecture, which consists of $n$ fusion blocks and $m$ ordinary blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-134: For the input point cloud $\mathbf{P}\in \mathbb R^{N\times C_{in }$, with $N$ points and $C_{in}$ channels, a sparse tensor $\mathcal{X}$ is obtained after voxelization, which can be expressed as a collection of 3D coordinates and non-empty voxel features, i.e., $\mathcal{X =\{\mathbf{F _V,\mathbf{C _V\}$, where $\mathbf{F _V\in \mathbb R^{N_V\times C_{in }$ represents the features of $N_V$ non-empty voxels and $\mathbf{C _V\in \mathbb R^{N_V\times 3}$ represents the coordinate of these voxels.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: After obtaining $\mathcal{X}$, it is passed through several fusion blocks and ordinary blocks.
elements.sent-2411-137: An ordinary block is a stage in a commonly used sparse backbone such as SECOND\cite{yanSECONDSparselyEmbedded2018a and VoxelNeXt\cite{chenVoxelNeXtFullySparse2023a , which is generally composed of a sparse convolution layer used for downsampling and several submanifold convolution layers or residual blocks.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: The input of an ordinary block is a sparse tensor $\mathcal{X _{in}$, and the output is another sparse tensor $\mathcal{X _{out}$ with the same or downsampled spatial shape.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:fusion_block , the fusion block can be regarded as an extension of the ordinary block.}
elements.sent-2411-140: It provides an extra operation between the sparse convolution layer and the residual blocks to retrieve multi-scale image features provided by the image branch.
elements.sent-2411-141: Specifically, we first compute the centroids of non-empty voxels in the sparse tensor $\mathcal{X _{sc}$ output by the sparse convolution.}
elements.sent-2411-142: That is, for the $k$-th non-empty voxel $\mathcal{V _k$ with feature $\mathbf{f _{vox,k}$, assuming $\mathcal{P _k=\{\mathbf{p _j\}, \mathbf{p _j\in \mathbb R^3, j=1,\cdots,|\mathcal{P _k|$ is the points located in $\mathcal{V _k$, the centroid $\mathbf{c _k \in \mathbb R^3$ can be obtained by \begin{equation}\mathbf{c}_k=\frac{1}{|\mathcal{P}_k|}\sum_{j=1}^{|\mathcal{P}_k|}\mathbf{p}_j,\end{equation} where $|\cdot|$ represents taking the cardinality of a set.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: Note that ``non-empty voxel'' refers to a voxel with non-zero features in the sparse tensor, and its inferior may not necessarily contain radar points which is due to the dilation effect of sparse convolution\cite{chenFocalSparseConvolutional2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Thus, for non-empty voxel $\mathcal{V _k$ with no internal points, i.e., $|\mathcal{P _k|=0$, we use its center instead.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: For convenience, we refer to them as centroid as well.
elements.sent-2411-146: We omit the subscript $k$ in the following, which defaults to a single non-empty voxel.
elements.sent-2411-147: The centroid of each non-empty voxel is then projected onto the image according to the camera intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{T _{intr \in \mathbb R^{3\times4}$ and the radar-to-camera coordinate transformation matrix $\mathbf{T _{r2c \in \mathbb R^{4\times4}$, which can be formulated as \begin{equation}\mathbf{c}_{img}' = \mathbf{T}_{intr} \cdot \mathbf{T}_{r2c} \cdot \mathbf{c}',\end{equation} where $\mathbf{c '=[\mathbf{c ; 1]\in \mathbb R^4$ represents the homogeneous coordinates of $\mathbf{c}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: As a result, $\mathbf{c _{img '=[ud,vd,d]^T$ is the homogeneous coordinate in the image coordinate system where $(u,v)$ and $d$ denote the pixel index and the corresponding depth, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Given the pixel index $\mathbf{c _{img =[u,v]^T$, the normalized coordinate can be obtained by $\tilde{\mathbf{c _{img = [u/W, v/H]^T\in \mathbb R^{[0,1]\times[0,1]}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: After obtaining the projection points for each non-empty voxel, image features are then retrieved from the multi-scale image feature maps by operator $\mathcal{E}$.
elements.sent-2411-151: Generally, the inputs of $\mathcal{E}$ are the normalized coordinate $\tilde{\mathbf{c _{img}$, multi-scale image features $\{\mathbf{F _{I,i}\}_{i=1 ^{n_I}$, and other useful input like queries denoted as $\mathbf{u}\in \mathbb R^{C_u}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: The output is the corresponding image feature $\mathbf{f _{img}\in \mathbb R^{C_{img }$, i.e., \begin{equation}\mathbf{f}_{img} = \mathcal{E}(\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{img}, \{\mathbf{F}_{I,i}\}_{i=1}^{n_I}, \mathbf{u}).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: The image feature is then fused with the corresponding voxel feature $\mathbf{f _{vox}$ by operator $\mathcal{F _{VI}$, obtaining the fused feature $\mathbf{f _{fuse}\in \mathbb R^{C_{fuse }$, that is \begin{equation}\mathbf{f}_{fuse}=\mathcal{F}_{VI}(\mathbf{f}_{img}, \mathbf{f}_{vox}).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The fused sparse tensor $\mathcal{X _{fuse}$ containing both voxel and image information can be constructed by simply replacing $\mathbf{f _{vox}$ to $\mathbf{f _{fuse}$ in $\mathcal{X _{sc}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Finally, $\mathcal{X _{fuse}$ is passed through the residual block to further process and fuse neighborhood features, obtaining the final output $\mathcal{X _{out}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Thus, the operators $\mathcal{E}$ and $\mathcal{F _{VI}$ do not change the feature dimension of $\mathcal{X _{sc}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: The above is a general strategy and no specific implementations of $\mathcal{E}$ and $\mathcal{F _{VI}$ are given.}
elements.sent-2411-159: In this study, we propose two implementations of the operator $\mathcal{E}$, i.e., simple feature fusion (SFF) and multi-scale deformable feature fusion (MSDFF).
elements.sent-2411-160: For $\mathcal{F _{VI}$, we simply adopt addition, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{equ:fusion_operator} \mathbf{f}_{fuse}=\mathcal{F}_{VI}(\mathbf{f}_{img}, \mathbf{f}_{vox})=\mathbf{f}_{img}+\mathbf{f}_{vox}.\end{equation}.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \subfloat[][SFF]{\includegraphics[width=3.5in]{simple_fusion_block_shape.pdf} \label{fig:simple_fusion_block}} \hfil \subfloat[][MSDFF]{\includegraphics[width=3.5in]{fusion_block_shape.pdf} \label{fig:deform_fusion_block}} \caption{Two types of fusion blocks. (a) shows the fusion block based on the SFF. The sparse tensor $\mathcal{X}_{n}$ with spatial shape $(D^{V}_{n}, H^{V}_{n}, W^{V}_{n})$ output by the previous block is fed to a sparse convolution layer with stride 2. The centroid of each non-empty voxel (use the center instead if the centroid is not available) is projected onto the image. Image features are then sampled from the multi-scale image feature maps through bilinear interpolation. After concatenation and mapping, the sampled feature $\mathbf{f}_{img}$ is fused with the voxel feature $\mathbf{f}_{vox}$ through $\mathcal{F}_{VI}$, obtaining $\mathbf{f}_{fuse}$. The final output is obtained after several residual blocks. (b) shows the fusion block based on the MSDFF. Unlike (a), for a non-empty voxel, the corresponding query $\mathbf{q}$ is first passed through two parallel linear layers to obtain sampling offsets and weights. Image features are sampled from the multi-scale image feature maps according to the sampling offsets. After the weighted summation and fusion operator, the fused feature $\mathbf{f}_{fuse}$ is obtained. Other processes are consistent with (a).} \label{fig:fusion_block}
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Fusion Block Based on the SFF
elements.sent-2411-161: The detailed process of the SFF is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:fusion_block (a).}
elements.sent-2411-162: According to the normalized image coordinate $\tilde{\mathbf{c _{img}$ projected from the centroid $\mathbf{c}$, we can use bilinear interpolation to sample image features from the multi-scale image feature maps.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: For the $i$-th level, we can obtain the sampled feature $\mathbf{f _{img ^i$ by \begin{equation}\label{equ:simple_sample} \mathbf{f}_{img}^i={\tt{Sample}}(\mathbf{F}_{I,i}, \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{img}),\end{equation} which can be accomplished using the ``$\tt{grid\_sample}$'' operation in Pytorch.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: The final image feature can be obtained by concatenating image features from all levels, and applying a linear project with batch normalization.
elements.sent-2411-165: The SFF operation is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{f}_{img} = \mathcal{E}_{SFF}(\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{img}, \{\mathbf{F}_{I,i}\}_{i=1}^{n_I}) = {\tt{Linear}_{BN}} ({\tt{Cat}}(\{\mathbf{f}_{img}^i\}_{i=1}^{n_I})). \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Fusion Block Based on the MSDFF
elements.sent-2411-166: Simple feature fusion can only focus on a single location for each projected point, resulting in a limited receptive field.
elements.sent-2411-167: To utilize the surrounding information of objects, increase the receptive field, and further enhance the ability of the feature extraction operator $\mathcal{E}$, we propose multi-scale deformable feature fusion, i.e., MSDFF, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:fusion_block (b).}
elements.sent-2411-168: The MSDFF is based on multi-scale deformable cross-attention\cite{zhuDeformableDETRDeformable2021a which can select different sample positions and adjust the weight of sampled features according to the corresponding query.}
elements.sent-2411-169: The process can be divided into two steps: query generation and deformable cross-attention.
elements.sent-2411-170: Extra queries are needed to guide the sampling process, and the queries are expected to have rich image and point cloud information to better select the desired features on the image feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-171: To meet this requirement, we add a query initialization module before the first fusion block to get high-quality queries.
elements.sent-2411-172: Specifically, for each reference point, we initialize the query as follows \begin{equation}\label{equ:init_query} \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{q}_{init} = {\tt{Linear_{BN}}}({\tt{Cat}}(\{\mathbf{f}_{vox}, \mathbf{f}_{img}^1, \mathbf{p}\})),\end{equation} where $\mathbf{p \in \mathbb R^{N\times 3}$ is the normalized center coordinate of the corresponding voxel.}
elements.sent-2411-173: As can be seen from Eq. (\ref{equ:init_query ), the initial query $\mathbf{q _{init}$ combines information from three sources.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: Among them, the voxel feature $\mathbf{f _{vox}$ provides radar information like RCS and velocity.}
elements.sent-2411-175: The normalized center coordinate $\mathbf{p}$ indicates the position and distance of the corresponding voxel.
elements.sent-2411-176: As for $\mathbf{f _{img ^1$, it is obtained by sampling the image feature in $\mathbf{F _{I,1}$ following Eq. (\ref{equ:simple_sample ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: As a result, $\mathbf{q _{init}$ contains rich information from both modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-178: For the subsequent fusion block, we directly use $\mathbf{f _{vox}$ as the query, since it already contains the previously fused image information, which means $\mathbf{q =\mathbf{f _{vox}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Guided by the query, deformable cross-attention is employed for the sampling process.
elements.sent-2411-180: For clarity, only single-head attention is considered below.
elements.sent-2411-181: Suppose we have $n_I$ image feature maps and want to sample $n_s$ features from each feature map for one query.
elements.sent-2411-182: The query $\mathbf{q}$ is passed through two parallel linear layers to obtain offsets $\{\mathbf{o _{i,j =(o_{i,j ^x,o_{i,j ^y)\}, \mathbf{o _{i,j}\in \mathbb R^{2}$ and weights $\{w_{i,j}\}, w_{i,j}\in \mathbb R$, where $i=1,\cdots, n_I$ and $j=1,\cdots,n_{s}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: The offsets are then normalized by the width and height of the corresponding image feature map, obtaining $\{\tilde{\mathbf{o _{i,j =(o_{i,j ^x/W_i,o_{i,j ^y/H_i)\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: For the $i$-th feature map, the $j$-th normalized sampling position $\tilde{\mathbf{c _{i,j ^s$ is calculated according to the reference point $\tilde{\mathbf{c _{img}$ and the offset $\tilde{\mathbf{o _{i,j}$ by \begin{equation}\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{i,j}^s = \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{img} + \tilde{\mathbf{o}}_{i,j}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Then, we sample features from the corresponding image feature level by bilinear interpolation following Eq. (\ref{equ:simple_sample ) and weighted summation is performed according to the normalized weight $\{\tilde{w _{i,j}\}={\tt Softmax (\{w_{i,j}\})$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: After applying a linear project with batch normalization, the image feature $\mathbf{f _{img}$ is obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-187: To sum up, the MSDFF operation can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{f}_{img} = \mathcal{E}_{MSDFF}(\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{img}, \{\mathbf{F}_{I,i}\}_{i=1}^{n_I}, \mathbf{q}) = {\tt{Linear_{BN}}} (\sum_{i=1}^{n_I}\sum_{j=1}^{n_{s}}\tilde{w}_{i,j}\cdot {\tt{Sample}}(\mathbf{F}_{I,i}, \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_{i,j}^s)). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Through the above method, the point cloud features are deeply interacted with the image features, so that the fused features contain rich geometric and semantic information, provided by the point clouds and images, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-188: These features help the network distinguish 3D foreground points and alleviate the feature-blurring problem.
elements.sent-2411-189: To better utilize the fused features and further mitigate the feature-blurring problem, we apply the semantic-guided head to the output of the last fusion block.
elements.sent-2411-190: In particular, for each non-empty voxel, we pass its feature $\mathbf{f _{fuse}$ through a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) and obtain the corresponding foreground score $s_{seg}$ to predict whether this voxel is a foreground one, i.e., \begin{equation}s_{seg} = {\tt{Sigmoid}}({\tt{MLP}}(\mathbf{f}_{fuse})).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-191: A foreground voxel is defined as a non-empty voxel whose centroid is in a 3D ground truth bounding box.
elements.sent-2411-192: Otherwise, we define it as a background voxel.
elements.sent-2411-193: Focal Loss\cite{linFocalLossDense2017 is used for explicit supervision.}
elements.sent-2411-194: When the score of each non-empty voxel is obtained, the voxel features are further multiplied by the score to guide the network to pay more attention to 3D foreground points.
elements.sent-2411-195: The weighted feature is obtained by \begin{equation}\mathbf{f}_{fuse}' = \mathbf{f}_{fuse}\cdot s_{seg}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-196: It should be noted that we mainly focus on the voxel-image fusion backbone, especially the fusion strategy.
elements.sent-2411-197: The 3D neck and the detection head are not limited to specific methods.
elements.sent-2411-198: For our voxel version, we adopt the methodology of VoxelNeXt\cite{chenVoxelNeXtFullySparse2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-199: The following is a brief introduction.
elements.sent-2411-200: Please refer to \cite{chenVoxelNeXtFullySparse2023a for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Given the sparse tensor $\mathcal{X _{out,i =\{\mathbf{F _{V,i ,\mathbf{C _{V,i}\}$ output by the block $i$, the 3D neck combines the non-empty voxels contained in $\{\mathcal{X _{out,4 , \mathcal{X _{out,5 , \mathcal{X _{out,6}\}$.}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-206: To facilitate the introduction of the proposed fusion module, the descriptions above are based on the voxel-based backbone.
elements.sent-2411-207: But our approach can be easily extended to lightweight yet powerful pillar-based networks with slightly adaptation.

elements.sent-2411-209: The major difference from the voxel version is that, in the pillar version, the intermediate features in the backbone are 2D BEV features rather than 3D sparse tensors.
elements.sent-2411-210: This difference does not affect the application of our fusion methodology.
elements.sent-2411-211: Specifically, for pillar-based networks, the input point cloud is typically pillarized before being fed into a BEV backbone with several conventional blocks.
elements.sent-2411-212: We replace the first $n$ blocks with our fusion block, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:pillar_version .}
elements.sent-2411-213: For the BEV feature output by a conventional block, the original point cloud can be voxelized with the same X and Y-resolution.
elements.sent-2411-214: For any non-empty voxel $\mathcal{V}$ with coordinates $(x,y,z)$, we can index the feature from the corresponding BEV feature map based on $(x,y)$.
elements.sent-2411-215: This feature can be directly lifted as the voxel's feature.
elements.sent-2411-216: However, to preserve height information, we introduce a learnable height embedding.
elements.sent-2411-217: The final voxel feature $\mathbf{f _{vox}$ is obtained by adding the corresponding BEV feature and the height embedding, expressed as \begin{equation}\mathbf{f}_{vox} = {\tt{BEV}}(x, y) + {\tt{HeightEmb}}(z),\end{equation} where ${\tt{BEV (x, y)$ represents the BEV feature at $(x, y)$, and ${\tt{HeightEmb (z)$ represents the height embedding corresponding to height $z$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: In this way, the BEV feature is effectively lifted into the non-empty voxels within 3D space.
elements.sent-2411-219: The SFF and MSDFF described above can then be used to aggregate features from images and fuse them into the non-empty voxels.
elements.sent-2411-220: Then, the voxel features with identical $(x,y)$ coordinates are summed to produce the fused BEV feature.
elements.sent-2411-221: Similar to the voxel-based version, we also employ a semantic-guided head after the last fusion block.
elements.sent-2411-222: Finally, the fused BEV feature pass through the remaining convolutional blocks, followed by the 2D neck and detection head, to produce the final detection results.
elements.sent-2411-223: Compared with the loss of the single-modal 3D object detection network, our method includes an additional segmentation loss in the semantic-guided head.
elements.sent-2411-224: Assuming $L_{seg}$ is the segmentation loss based on Focal Loss\cite{linFocalLossDense2017 , and $L_{det}$ is the detection loss corresponding to the single-modal method\cite{chenVoxelNeXtFullySparse2023a}\cite{langPointPillarsFastEncoders2019a , which is generally composed of classification, location and other losses depending on the specific method.}}

elements.sent-2411-227: We use two datasets to evaluate our model, i.e., the VoD dataset\cite{palffyMultiClassRoadUser2022 and the TJ4DRadset dataset\cite{zhengTJ4DRadSet4DRadar2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-228: Both of them are oriented toward autonomous driving applications, especially for 4D radar perception.
elements.sent-2411-229: The VoD dataset has a total of 8682 frames, including 5139 frames in the training set, 1296 frames in the validation set, and 2247 frames in the test set.
elements.sent-2411-230: Since the official evaluation system is not yet open, our comparison and ablation experiments were completed on the validation set.
elements.sent-2411-231: The VoD dataset is collected in the city of Delft (The Netherlands) and covers campus, suburb and old-town scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-232: It provides synchronized 4D radar, LiDAR, camera, and GPU/IMU data with 3D annotations and tracking IDs.
elements.sent-2411-233: Moreover, the official also provides radar point clouds accumulated from multiple scans, which is implemented by compensating ego-motion.
elements.sent-2411-234: Following previous works\cite{liuSMURFSpatialMultiRepresentation2023a}\cite{xiongLXLLiDARExcluded2023b}\cite{zhengRCFusionFusing4D2023 , we use the five-scan radar points and consider three categories, i.e., car, pedestrian, and cyclist.}
elements.sent-2411-235: The TJ4DRadset dataset was collected in Suzhou, China, covering different road types, such as urban roads, elevated roads and industrial zones.
elements.sent-2411-236: Compared with the VoD dataset, it contains more difficult scenarios, e.g., nighttime, glare, under the bridge, and wrong camera focus, which are big challenges for the camera.
elements.sent-2411-237: It has a total of 7746 frames, of which 5706 frames are for training and 2040 frames for testing.
elements.sent-2411-238: Its sensor configuration is similar to that in the VoD dataset, but it only provides synchronized 4D radar and camera data, with 3D annotations and tracking IDs up to now.
elements.sent-2411-239: For the TJ4DRadset dataset, we adopt single-frame radar data without accumulation and consider four categories, i.e., car, pedestrian, cyclist, and truck.
elements.sent-2411-240: Both the VoD and TJ4DRadSet datasets use averaging precision (AP) as the main evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-241: For the VoD dataset, according to the official recommendations, two metrics are used, i.e., AP under the entire annotated area ($\text{AP _\text{EAA}$) and AP under the driving corridor ($\text{AP _\text{DC}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-242: The former means that all annotations are used for evaluation regardless of range.
elements.sent-2411-243: The latter means that we only consider annotations located in a specific area which is defined as $\mathcal{A _{DC =\{(x,y,z)|-4\text{m <x<4\text{m , z<25\text{m}\}$ in camera coordinates.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-244: When calculating AP, the intersection-over-union (IoU) threshold is set to 0.25 for cyclists and pedestrians, and 0.5 for cars.
elements.sent-2411-245: The IoU threshold is used to determine positive and negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-246: For the TJ4DRadset dataset, 3D AP ($\text{AP _\text{3D}$) and BEV AP ($\text{AP _\text{BEV}$) are evaluated with a uniform range of 0-70m, and the IoU thresholds are set in line with the VoD dataset, with an IoU threshold of 0.5 for the additional truck category.}}
elements.sent-2411-247: We implement our model based on MMDetection3D\cite{mmdet3d2020 framework which is an open-source 3D object detection toolbox based on PyTorch.}
elements.sent-2411-248: For the VoD dataset, following official configurations, we set the point cloud range to $\{(x,y,z)|0\text{m <x<51.2\text{m ,-25.6\text{m <y<25.6\text{m ,-3\text{m <z<2\text{m}\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-249: We use the radar point cloud accumulated over 5 scans as input.
elements.sent-2411-250: The input feature is selected as \begin{equation}\mathbf{f}_{in}^{\text{VoD}} = [x, y, z, RCS, v_r, v_{rc}, t]^T,\end{equation} where $RCS$ represents the radar cross section reflecting the reflection intensity of the object, $v_r$ is the relative radial Doppler velocity, $v_{rc}$ is the absolute radial Doppler velocity, and $t$ is the time ID, indicating which scan it originates from.

elements.paragraph-2411-012: Effects of Different Fusion Blocks and \# Fusion Stages
elements.sent-2411-369: We conduct two groups of experiments to observe the performance difference between the two proposed fusion blocks and the impact of the number of fusion blocks.
elements.sent-2411-370: In the first group, we adopt the MSDFF-based fusion block and fix $n+m=6$.
elements.sent-2411-371: The number of fusion blocks $n$ increases from 0 to 4.
elements.sent-2411-372: When $n=0$, it degenerates into a single-modal baseline.
elements.sent-2411-373: In the second group, we choose the fusion block based on SFF, and the other experimental settings are the same as those of the first group.
elements.sent-2411-374: The experiment results are shown in Table \ref{tab:fusion_block .}
elements.sent-2411-375: For better comparison, we also list the mAP calculated at stricter IoU thresholds, i.e., $\text{mAP _{\text{str }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-376: In particular, the IoU thresholds corresponding to the categories of cars, pedestrians, and cyclists are 0.7, 0.5, and 0.5, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-377: For the first group, the detection performance compared to the baseline has been significantly improved, when only one fusion block is used.
elements.sent-2411-378: When the number of fusion blocks gradually increases, mAP does not increase significantly.
elements.sent-2411-379: On the contrary, there is a slight decrease in AP for the cyclist category.
elements.sent-2411-380: This is because the rich image features make it easy for the network to mistake distant cyclists as pedestrians.
elements.sent-2411-381: For cyclists who are far away or blocked, the bicycle is easily confused with the background, and the rider above is easier to detect.
elements.sent-2411-382: In addition, the deeper the block, the larger the corresponding voxel volume, causing the image features sampled by its centroid no longer represent the entire voxel accurately, especially when $n=4$.
elements.sent-2411-383: However, it should be noticed that as $n$ increases, $\text{mAP _{\text{str }$ improves.}}
elements.sent-2411-384: This result indicates that sufficient image features and deep fusion can help object positioning.
elements.sent-2411-385: This makes sense for radar, as radar point clouds lack accurate geometric information of objects due to sparsity and noisiness.
elements.sent-2411-386: Nevertheless, information like object orientation is easier to perceive in images.
elements.sent-2411-387: More accurate object localization can be achieved through deep interaction of voxel and image features.
elements.sent-2411-388: Furthermore, as the fusion block has higher computational costs than the ordinary block, FPS also decreases slightly, as $n$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-389: The decrease in FPS is slight thanks to the sparsity of radar point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-390: Interestingly, the improvement for the second group is not as obvious as that in the first group when $n=1$.
elements.sent-2411-391: This is because the MSDFF has a stronger image feature extraction ability than SFF.
elements.sent-2411-392: The MSDFF can adaptively focus on the desired image features depending on the query.
elements.sent-2411-393: As $n$ increases (except $n=4$), both mAP and $\text{mAP _\text{str}$ increase.}
elements.sent-2411-394: In particular, the improvement of $\text{mAP _\text{str}$ is obvious, which is consistent with the first group.}
elements.sent-2411-395: When $n=2,3$, it even surpasses the first group under the EAA.
elements.sent-2411-396: This is because multi-stage feature fusion drowns out the advantages of MSDFF.
elements.sent-2411-397: In addition, the increase in voxel volume leads to unrepresentative image features as mentioned above, which also weakens the effect of MSDFF.
elements.sent-2411-398: In summary, both groups of results illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed multi-stage sampling, especially under tighter IoU thresholds.

elements.sent-2411-401: It is observed that when the semantic-guided head is not used, that is, neither SL nor SW is used, $\text{mAP _\text{EAA}$ decreases by 2.1\% and 1.3\% for $n=1$ and $3$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-402: Despite this, the detection performance without the semantic-guided head is still significantly better than the single-modal baselines.
elements.sent-2411-403: The reason is that the classification loss in the detection head plays a similar role to SL, which can help the model identify 3D foreground points based on the fused BEV feature map with radar and image information.

elements.sent-2411-416: Under the same hardware and input resolution, our method achieves higher inference speed and comparable FLOPs while delivering significantly better detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-417: Notably, the FLOPs of the BEV pooling operation in BEVFusion are not included, despite its non-negligible time cost, which reaches approximately 20ms at our resolution.
elements.sent-2411-418: In contrast, our method does not involve explicit view transformation but instead uses sampling, with MSDFF requiring only about 2ms under the same conditions.
elements.sent-2411-419: In this study, we proposed a simple but efficient 4D radar and camera fusion network, namely MSSF, for 3D object detection.
elements.sent-2411-420: Specifically, we designed two plug-and-play fusion blocks to effectively utilize and fuse image features, which alleviate the feature-blurring problem in image feature sampling.
elements.sent-2411-421: Through the cascade of multiple fusion blocks, the detection performance is further improved, especially in terms of the localization accuracy of bounding boxes, which is attributed to the deep interaction between voxel features and image features.
elements.sent-2411-422: Moreover, we devised a semantic-guided head to guide the network to explicitly perceive foreground points, thereby further mitigating the feature-blurring problem and enhancing performance.
elements.sent-2411-423: The effectiveness of our method has been verified on the VoD and TJ4DRadset datasets, significantly surpassing existing methods and achieving new state-of-the-art performance.
elements.sent-2411-424: Notably, MSSF even outperforms the classic LiDAR-based models.
elements.sent-2411-425: Note that our MSSF provides a concise and effective architecture based on the fusion of 4D radar and camera.
elements.sent-2411-426: It can inspire subsequent work and remind upcoming efforts to pay attention to the feature-blurring problem.
elements.sent-2411-427: Future work may involve leveraging temporal information and the depth information estimated from images for better matching radar points and image pixels, thus eliminating feature blurring and improving detection capabilities.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
